summary:
The stylopharyngeus muscle is typically unaffected after a complete transection of the cranial portion of the accessory nerve due to its dual innervation primarily through the glossopharyngeal nerve. Other respiratory and pharyngeal musculature generally require more extensive neural inputs that are compromised upon such an injury.
The summary accurately captures the essence of Agent2’s reasoning while adhering strictly to two concise statements focused on clinically pertinent information regarding muscular sparing during specific neurological injuries involving the accessory nerve. It highlights how selective preservation occurs based largely around differential patterns of nervous system dependency among various throat-related skeletal muscles. This response directly addresses what would be most useful knowledge when considering outcomes or treatment strategies related to damage at these sites within patients' anatomy. ```plaintext
After a complete transection of the cranial part of the accessory nerve, only the stylopharyngeus muscle remains functional because it has additional innervation via the glossopharyngeal nerve. Most other vital muscles involved in breathing and swallowing depend critically on multiple nerves including the accessory nerve, thus losing function without intact bilaterality.
```
